title: Topic 236: Why Players Ignore Tutorials: The Psychology of Impatience
content:
# Topic 236: Why Players Ignore Tutorials: The Psychology of Impatience

## ABSTRACT

This paper investigates the pervasive and often self-defeating phenomenon of players skipping video game tutorials. While seemingly a trivial act of impatience, this behavior offers a profound glimpse into the intricate workings of the human psyche, particularly in the context of consumer behavior and instant gratification. Through a rigorous, albeit humorous, analysis of psychological principles, real-world gaming examples, and existing academic literature, this paper argues that the propensity to ignore tutorials is not merely a sign of a short attention span but a complex interplay of cognitive biases, motivational drives, and the very design of modern interactive entertainment. We explore the delicate balance game developers must strike between effective instruction and the player's insatiable desire to "just play the game already." The findings presented herein have significant implications for user experience design, marketing, and anyone who has ever tried to assemble IKEA furniture without looking at the instructions.

## INTRODUCTION

The year is 2026. You’ve just downloaded the most anticipated game of the decade, a sprawling epic that promises hundreds of hours of immersive gameplay. The title screen fades, the music swells, and a gentle, paternal voice begins to guide you through the basic controls. "Press X to jump," it says. But do you listen? Of course not. You hammer every button on the controller, spin the analog sticks in a furious dance of defiance, and desperately search for the 'skip' button. You are a modern gamer, and you have no time for such pedestrian hand-holding. You are, in a word, impatient.

This paper delves into the heart of this curious phenomenon, exploring the psychological underpinnings of why players, with an almost religious fervor, ignore tutorials. It is a behavior that transcends genre, platform, and player demographic, uniting the grizzled veteran and the fresh-faced newbie in a shared pact of instructional disobedience. We will examine how the very nature of video games, with their promise of immediate feedback and reward, has cultivated a culture of impatience. We will also explore the cognitive biases at play, from the Dunning-Kruger effect, which convinces us we know more than we do, to the simple, primal urge for autonomy and control. Through a blend of academic rigor and comedic observation, this paper will not only shed light on a common gaming quirk but also offer a broader commentary on the modern consumer's relationship with technology and information.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not entirely overlooked the curious case of the tutorial-skipping gamer. While the body of research is not as vast as, say, studies on the effects of caffeine on graduate students, there are nonetheless some valuable insights to be gleaned. A seminal, if somewhat obscure, paper by Andersen et al. (2012) from the prestigious *Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems* (a title that just rolls off the tongue) found that tutorials can, in fact, increase playtime and player performance, but only in games of sufficient complexity. In simpler games, the tutorial's effect was negligible, suggesting that players have a reasonably accurate, if subconscious, sense of when they need help and when they can just wing it. This aligns with the findings of Cao and Liu (2022), who, in their thrilling paper "Learning to play: understanding in-game tutorials with a pilot study on implicit tutorials," discovered that experienced gamers, in particular, benefit from more subtle, "implicit" tutorials that are woven into the gameplay itself, rather than presented as a separate, mandatory lesson. This suggests that the more we play, the more we develop a kind of "gamer's intuition," a sixth sense that allows us to sniff out a tutorial from a mile away and take evasive action.

Of course, no discussion of impatience would be complete without mentioning the work of Ghafurian (2017), whose doctoral dissertation, "Impatience in Dynamic Decision-Making," used a game to study timing decisions. While not directly about tutorials, the research highlights the irrationality that can creep into our choices when we're in a hurry. This is the same irrationality that leads us to believe we can master a complex flight simulator without so much as a glance at the manual, only to find ourselves in a nosedive five seconds after takeoff. The literature, in its dry, academic way, is telling us what we already know in our hearts: we are an impatient bunch, and we will go to great lengths to avoid being told what to do, even when it's for our own good.

## ANALYSIS

So, what is the psychological cocktail that fuels this rampant tutorial-phobia? At its core lies a potent blend of cognitive biases, motivational drives, and the very design of the medium itself. Let's dissect this potent brew, shall we?

First and foremost, we have the **Dunning-Kruger effect**, that glorious cognitive bias that leads people of low ability to overestimate their competence. In the context of gaming, this manifests as the unshakeable belief that one's years of experience playing *Super Mario Bros.* have endowed them with the innate ability to master any game, no matter how complex. The player, convinced of their own gaming prowess, sees the tutorial not as a helpful guide but as an insulting insinuation that they are, in fact, a complete and utter novice. To accept the tutorial would be to admit that they are not the gaming god they believe themselves to be, a blow to the ego that is simply too great to bear.

Then there is the powerful drive for **autonomy**. As articulated by Self-Determination Theory, humans have a fundamental need to feel in control of their own actions and decisions. A mandatory, unskippable tutorial is the antithesis of autonomy. It is a digital taskmaster, forcing the player down a narrow, predetermined path, robbing them of the very freedom and agency that games are supposed to provide. The act of skipping the tutorial, then, becomes an act of rebellion, a declaration of independence from the tyranny of the game developer. "You can't tell me what to do!" the player cries, as they proceed to spend the next hour fumbling with the controls, utterly lost and confused.

Finally, we must consider the role of **instant gratification**. Video games are masters of the dopamine drip, providing a steady stream of rewards and positive feedback that keeps us hooked. We are conditioned to expect immediate results. We press a button, something happens. We kill a monster, we get experience points. The tutorial, with its delayed gratification, its promise of future competence in exchange for present-day patience, is a jarring interruption to this cycle. It is the video game equivalent of being told to eat your vegetables before you can have dessert. And, as any parent can attest, that is a battle that is often lost.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The annals of gaming history are littered with tales of tutorial-related woe. Consider the infamous "jetpack incident" from the *Tribes: Ascend* beta, as chronicled in the hallowed halls of Reddit. A player, so enamored with the game's promise of high-speed, jetpack-fueled combat, convinced his friends to join him. Yet, in their haste to experience the aerial ballet of death, they all skipped the tutorial. The result? A chorus of frustrated groans as they repeatedly plummeted to their doom, unable to figure out the game's most fundamental mechanic. The very thing that had drawn them to the game in the first place became a source of immense frustration, all because they couldn't be bothered to spend five minutes in a tutorial.

Or take the case of *Diablo III*, a game that was met with a firestorm of criticism upon its release. While many of the complaints were legitimate, a significant portion of the player base seemed to be blissfully unaware of certain game mechanics that were, in fact, explained in the tutorial. The game was accused of being too simple, too streamlined, yet many of these same players were neglecting to use the very systems that would have added the depth and complexity they craved. It was a classic case of blaming the game for one's own instructional negligence.

These are not isolated incidents. They are a testament to a universal truth: players will, with a consistency that is almost admirable, choose the path of immediate, unguided struggle over the path of patient, guided mastery. It is a choice that is as irrational as it is human.

## DISCUSSION

The implications of this tutorial-skipping epidemic extend far beyond the world of gaming. They offer a fascinating window into the modern consumer's psyche. We live in an age of instant everything, where a world of information is just a click away. Yet, we are often too impatient to access it. We want the results without the process, the knowledge without the learning. This has profound consequences for everything from marketing to education.

For game developers, the challenge is to design tutorials that are so seamlessly integrated into the gameplay experience that the player doesn't even realize they're being taught. The best tutorials are invisible, teaching through level design, environmental cues, and a gradual introduction of new mechanics. They respect the player's intelligence and their desire for autonomy, while still providing the necessary guidance. It is a delicate balancing act, a tightrope walk between hand-holding and abandonment.

For marketers, the lesson is that the modern consumer has an attention span that can be measured in nanoseconds. You have a fleeting moment to capture their interest, to convey your message before they move on to the next shiny object. The hard sell, the lengthy explanation, the detailed instruction manual – these are relics of a bygone era. Today, it's all about the elevator pitch, the sound bite, the tweet.

## CONCLUSION

The act of skipping a video game tutorial is more than just a minor annoyance for game developers. It is a microcosm of the human condition in the 21st century. It is a story of impatience, of overconfidence, of our insatiable thirst for instant gratification. It is a story of our desire for autonomy, our rebellion against being told what to do, even when it is in our best interest. And so, the next time you find yourself impatiently mashing the 'skip' button, take a moment to reflect. You are not just a gamer in a hurry. You are a participant in a grand, ongoing experiment in human psychology. And you are, in all likelihood, about to get very, very lost.
references:
[1] Andersen, E., O'Rourke, E., Liu, Y. E., Snider, R., & Popović, Z. (2012, May). The impact of tutorials on games of varying complexity. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI conference on human factors in computing systems (pp. 59-68).

[2] Cao, S., & Liu, F. (2022). Learning to play: understanding in-game tutorials with a pilot study on implicit tutorials. Heliyon, 8(11), e11482.

[3] Ghafurian, M. (2017). Impatience in Dynamic Decision-Making: Its Moderation, and Implications for User Interface Design. The Pennsylvania State University.
